name: Hermes (Mercury in Roman mythology)
shortDescription: Hermes, known as Mercury in Roman mythology, is the messenger of the gods, a divine trickster, and guide of souls to the underworld. He is associated with commerce, eloquence, and travel.
description: Hermes, the Greek god, is known as Mercury in Roman mythology. He is the swift-footed messenger of the gods, son of Zeus (Jupiter) and Maia. Born in a cave on Mount Cyllene in Arcadia, he displayed his cunning from a young age, stealing Apollo's cattle shortly after his birth. This mischievous act led to a reconciliation with Apollo, resulting in Hermes gifting Apollo the lyre he had crafted from a tortoise shell. Hermes's responsibilities are vast and varied. He serves as a herald, delivering messages between gods and mortals. He guides souls of the dead to the underworld (Psychopompos) and is a patron of travelers, merchants, orators, and thieves. His symbols include the caduceus (a winged staff with two snakes intertwined), winged sandals (talaria), and a traveler's hat (petasos). Mercury, inheriting these traits in Roman mythology, played a similar role as a facilitator of communication and trade. He was particularly important to Roman merchants and was often depicted with a purse, representing wealth. Mercury's influence extended to various aspects of Roman life, and temples were dedicated to him throughout the Roman Empire. Both Hermes and Mercury represent the power of communication, intelligence, and adaptability.
